speaker: interviewer
text: This is the third tape of an interview with Uri Rantz on October 14, 1996. And you were describing closing down the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I forgot still, one thing to mention, that in 1946, in October, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got an order from Israel. Since the British were-- this is before the-- before the establishment of the state. And the British were closing [NON-ENGLISH] and arresting the people-- to blow up British embassy in Rome. We blew up the embassy with, with no victims. We make sure nobody's going to get hurt, especially no Italians. It was a old palazzo. We took out-- the whole center of the building came out. But nobody was hurt. And it was a big commotion in the papers at that time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this part of the-- being in the Irgun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irgun, yeah, this was the part that was the Irgun. And at the same time, other-- the groups in Austria, the Irgun in Austria, the Irgun in Germany, made all kinds of different military operations, like blowing up British trains, derailing them, and so on, and so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if I have to really talk about the work of the Irgun. But to make shortly that-- considering most of the people came from the camps or from Russia, no money, no conditions, and to build up from this system [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] an organization that was functional and efficient, it's only, I think, I don't know that any other nationality could, could do it. Maybe the work was also-- it's not so much that we did something for somebody else. Because I don't think this is the philosophy of it. The work made us come back to become-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to feel that we are equal or as good as anybody else. It was not that none of us didn't think that they're doing a sacrifice for anybody. This was to become the self-esteem, to, to get the self-esteem. And, and people did things without even questioning. They just-- their personal life was no problem. And because now we talk things that which-- in perspective. But while those things were happening, if the Russians would have caught us, many people disappeared. If the British would have caught us, we would end up in all kinds of prisons and all kind of-- they weren't so nice people, the British, towards the Jews. And-- but somehow-- and this is what-- this is that survival instinct. Or maybe I'm being too Jewish. I think, maybe because we Jews-- because I saw Germans who-- and Austrians, they could fight for a thousand years. They, they surrendered with-- practically right away. Jews, whenever they could fight, they fought. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then became a problem, since I remained in Italy. Because of my-- my job was the, the contact with the Italian authorities and other things. So in meantime, we were getting letters from Israel that other people cannot get a job, cannot get-- don't get housing because Ben-Gurion's Mapai government were discriminating against the ex-special-- against ex-Irgun or ex-Betar people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here comes in another side story. The Italians started a little bit to, to, to ask us, why do we stay so long in Italy? Many-- most of the people left. And we are staying, staying. So we decided to tell a story that we are applying for a visa to America. I could have a visa-- a visa from my uncles. I could have it in no time. But I didn't want an affidavit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted-- so-- and the guy who was in charge in the Joint Distribution of those things was our sympathizer. And we told him, you direct everything until the last moment. And so everything was dragging, dragging, by that time. And the bad news came from Israel. I had-- happened with [LAUGHS] a visa to Los Angeles. My sponsor was from Los Angeles. And, since I wanted to study, to-- so I figured-- I came to the United States, to Los Angeles. And from there, after two, three months, I came back to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles? My boat arrived to the States September 15, 1950. And actually, I-- we went to New Orleans. And from New Orleans, I-- we flew to Los Angeles. And I was probably, by the end of September-- September 20th, something, in the States. I was with a group of Sephardic Jews from Rome. And I was-- in December, I came to New York. My uncle had a dental lab. I started to work for my uncle. And since I wanted to go to college, I had to make the regents. I didn't have my paper. In three months, I made the Regents Examination here because many subjects, like mathematics and physics, I went cold, without, without even studying. And I applied-- like, I had very few options. I needed a college which was free of tuition. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Cooper Union, they told me, City College and Cooper Union. But it's more difficult, the Cooper Union. Since I wasn't a youngster-- so I said, I'll try Cooper Union. If I'm not good there, then I don't do it. Out of 400, they accepted a hundred. I was among the hundred. And I finished engineering. I had to do it at night because I had to work daytime. My father forgot to send the money to America. Anyhow, so soon I finished. I got a job, which was in '59 with Bendix in Teterboro in missiles-- inertial guidance systems, missiles. This was the time that the Russians put out the Sputnik. We were behind. And it was a big pressure. So I used to work sometimes two shifts because my tests used to take 10 hours. So-- and if you interrupt, you have to start from the beginning, so-- you see. But I liked what I was doing. And I was very interested, everything challenging. In my job, about the [? journals ?] and the books when I studied was maybe a third of a page describing a [INAUDIBLE] definition. And that's it. And everything is-- I worked in RD, at research and development. And part of the Pershing Guidance System I developed, since we didn't have yet. I worked there for three years. I had a very bad car accident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I broke my vessels in my-- a very bad accident. I was taken to the hospital. And I couldn't keep working. I got headaches. I had to quit engineering. Actually, I could do-- be there, but if-- but I didn't feel-- if I cannot do my job good, I don't belong. And I went to business. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we got to one business. We got the jewelry concessions in department stores. And eventually, we got-- with my partner-- a patent for watches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we built a factory in Israel. We, we manufactured watches all for export to the United States and to Europe. It was quite a nice factory. About 50 peoples were working there in [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the factory was Raga-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] watch, time-- Timex, Raga. And then we sold it to a Swiss company because it became too much absentee. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my first wife, it took us a long time because she had the Italian husband. In Italy there, even the Jews couldn't get a divorce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she wanted to go to Mexico. At that time, she got a letter from her lawyer that her husband died. So we got married in 1959 in Reno. And she died in '87 in Paris. We went for the unveiling of her cousin's grave. She caught pneumonia. And she's buried in Israel. And this was the same time, but it was 10 days later that my present wife's husband died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And God works in mysterious ways, I guess. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a very big-- she's buried among the-- in the, the-- in Holon in the cemeteries for the-- a special section for the fighters of Irgun. She was going by the name 'Dottoressa.' Everybody knew her. And it was a very, very big funeral, hundreds of people, all ex-Italian people who knew her from Italy, France, relatives, and such.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Begin wasn't to the funeral. But Begin was in our house in New York. And then we still lived in, in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after she died in Paris, I was ready to go to Israel. But things worked out that I got to-- with, with my present wife, we decided to get married. She is a very nice person. I knew her as long as she knew her-- all the years from when I took her through the border. I transferred her-- transferred them to the border. And all the years, we were meeting. And they lived in Italy. And I lived in Rome in Italy. They lived in Grottaferrata, which is the southern-- no, I'm sorry, they lived in Santa Cesarea, which is the southern-- next to Barrea-- the southern part of Italy. And I used to travel because our people were there, and I had business to do there, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the years we were here in America, we used to be spending weekends over in our place and their place and vacations together. And, and we should mention about the kids. Not that I knew them all my life-- this was a misrepresent-- all life. [LAUGHS] So this is a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if you have some questions? Did you ever go back to Lutzk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were told in 1987, '88-- we got a letter from Israel, that some people came from, from Lutsk to Israel. And they are going on that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place where the, the cave around there-- cemetery there, the 28,000 or 29,000 Jews are buried-- they're going to build a highway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, this was-- didn't make-- we were shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to congressmen-- I forgot now the names-- in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Except wishes, we couldn't get anything. There we met all kinds of Lubavitch [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] missionaries. They were going to Russia all the time, these Lubavitch. They were doing a tremendous job, the Lubavitch Hasidic. They give us a few hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we took ourselves. My friend, who's the president of-- also my classmate from gymnasium from Lutsk, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who is the president of the Volhynian Jews-- Federation of Volhynian Jews. And we went to, to Lutsk-- this was in 1988. Luckily, we met his-- he had a cousin that he didn't know exactly, didn't have contact with him, who was a hero of the Soviet Union. He was a pilot and a secretary of the Communist Party. This was during Gorbachev's time. And we just had a very good time. He took us to his friend, who was in charge of the museums of, of the state. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that guy was half-Jewish. He didn't feel Jewish at all, but his mother was Jewish. And the policy, the Russian policy changed at that time. So, in other words, first of all, we wanted to put a-- to stop the road and to put a-- not save it, to put a monument there. This was agreed not only for us, but for all the Jewish places in the whole state. The second thing, we asked for a permission to put in Jewish because, like, in Babi Yar, when we went the first time, the only-- here are buried-- Babi Yar is that famous mass grave of Jews in Kiev. Here-- it was written, 'Here are buried 120,000 Russian citizens.' We wanted to change-- to add 'Russian citizens of Jewish faith.' And the same thing, to put it in Jewish-- agreed. Then we would-- we, we asked once, we, we asked-- my father told me, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, 'I'm going to slap you if you ask that.' So we ask for Hebrew. Hebrew wasn't yet ready. This was still the-- Gorbachev's time. But anyhow, we had money for it. I mean, we had money. My friend and I decided we were going to pay for it. So the monument, they didn't want money because this was the government-- the government's decision to do it. It'll become like, a, a historic place. But what we added-- benches, marble benches, and, and gates, and so on. This we paid for ourselves. My friend and I, we paid for it. And then we did it. And then we arranged also with the guy in charge of the museums. We invited him to Israel. We paid for his trip because he was very cooperative. And we paid for the trip for his and his wife. And then we give him every year-- we gave him one lump sum. And then every year that he should take care of the grave-- to, to take care. So, in other words, we went in '88 to arrange things. In, in '89 was the unveiling. And happened a thing that we couldn't believe. In '88, when we came, we didn't have a minyan to say Kaddish. In '89, somehow, they decided to make a propaganda affair of it. So then we had television and radio. The secretary of the Communist Party spoke. The Secretary of Ukrainian spoke there. And they made to have hundreds of people-- we had-- we have some videos of it, that-- mountains of flowers-- schools, everybody was taking flowers for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and that's it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Who's going to visit? I don't know. But we had to be in peace with ourselves, so we did what we had to do. The future, nobody knows. Do you have a message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing which I would-- the way I said before that it would be survivors who are doing things to, to-- for themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One thing-- if people are strong in their Jewishness, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's nothing to do-- not directly religion because we can-- we can be nationalistic and not religious necessarily. First of all, they know why things are happening. The second thing-- you have a source to draw on to get encouragement, to get faith to do things, not just let-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to be-- to let-- to, to be abused. Of course, there are governments, and there are stronger people than we are in certain contexts. But the main thing, there is-- it cannot-- there is no such thing that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we cannot do it, or we have to do it exactly the same way. And-- but the basis is, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is we have to draw on our own strength, which is the-- our Jewish history, our Jewish religion, our Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me sits my wife, Fenia Rantz, a beautiful woman, a wonderful woman, a [NON-ENGLISH], and a good mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my grandfather, my mother's father. The name is Yisroel Juniczman. This picture is approximately taken in the early '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk. This picture was taken in 1927, after the wedding of my aunt, Ruchel, to her husband. And on this picture are my, my father's family and the family of my-- of the [NON-ENGLISH] side. Under my, my father's family is the grandmother, Leah. My father, my mother, the two uncles, and I am on the picture, and my younger brother. Another one, the third one, is my cousin. This picture is me when I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe one year old-- I don't know, maybe half-- six months-- and my parents, taken in Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- my mother's name is Genia. And my father's name is [NON-ENGLISH]. This picture is from the second or third grade, [NON-ENGLISH]. You have-- this is a class. This is all the kids from our class with the teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk. And it was taking probably approximately in the-- in '28 or '29. This is the-- this is the picture of the great synagogue in Lutsk, which exists until today. But there isn't a-- it is a-- it is now a-- offices, quarters of the soccer team of Lutsk. This was taken some-- in the '20s in Lutsk. This is the picture of the mass grave of 28,000 Jews of our town and surroundings. The group there-- there is a little sign-- there is a sign about the-- just about the grave. And the people there are survivors from our town and from towns around, around Lutsk, taken in 1945, '46. This picture is the same-- the same place where the other picture was taken, but in 1989, after we built a [HEBREW], a monument-- and a marble [HEBREW]. And these-- and these are the people who took part in the-- in the unveiling. This was in 1989. This picture was taken in 1946 in Grottaferrata, Italy, during the first Kinos, in other words, the first congress of Betar after the war. This is-- the picture was taken in Israel in front of Dan Hotel in, in the early '60s. In that picture is my late wife, Janet, and my friend, [NON-ENGLISH], who was one of the three that-- my friend from Israel came with-- we organized the Irgun together in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture is of my late wife and my late-- and of my present wife's late husband, taken, I would say, in the-- in front-- can I take a look at the picture? No. In Syosset.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Syosset and-- in the-- in the '70s. This picture was taken in 1946 in Rome, Italy. This is a, a correspondent identification. This helped me to go into a lot of places as a foreign correspondent. This is a, a medal received in the late-- in the '70s for the ex-members of the Irgun in Europe. This is a certificate issued to us, a small group of people, for their work in Europe and in the Brihah and the Irgun and, and the organizations, signed by Begin, Shamir, all the big names in the management and leadership of the Herut and Likud movement.
